edges.source: Intravenous Immunoglobulin for Steroid-Refractory or Pediatric Disease

nodes.description: Serum anti-thyroid peroxidase and/or anti-thyroglobulin antibodies are part of the SREAT case definition and reflect B-cell and plasma-cell autoimmune activity, but the antibodies are common in the general population and do not by themselves prove autoimmune CNS disease. This node is therefore modeled as a diagnostic and autoimmune-context mechanism rather than a direct neuronal injury mechanism.

evidence.reference_title: Steroid-responsive encephalopathy associated with autoimmune thyroiditis (SREAT): Characteristics, treatment and outcome in 251 cases from the literature.
evidence.snippet: In serum, 34% of patients were positive for anti-thyroid peroxidase (TPO) antibodies, 7% for anti-thyroglobulin (TG) antibodies, and 69% both.

evidence.reference_title: Brain dysfunction and thyroid antibodies: autoimmune diagnosis and misdiagnosis.
evidence.snippet: Thyroid antibody prevalence is high in the general population, and does not support a diagnosis of autoimmune encephalopathy in the absence of objective neurological and CNS-specific immunological abnormalities.

nodes.description: Corticosteroids are the usual first-line therapy for suspected SREAT after a reasonable exclusion of infectious, toxic-metabolic, neoplastic, neurodegenerative, psychiatric, and better-defined autoimmune encephalitis mimics. Response rates are high in selected cohorts, but steroid response is not universal and does not replace diagnostic rigor.

evidence.reference_title: Steroid-responsive encephalopathy associated with autoimmune thyroiditis (SREAT): Characteristics, treatment and outcome in 251 cases from the literature.
evidence.snippet: The first-line treatment was steroids in 193 patients

evidence.reference_title: Clinical presentations and treatment outcomes of Hashimoto encephalopathy at Siriraj Hospital
evidence.snippet: Among the 12 patients who received corticosteroid, 9 (75%) had marked improvement
Only 6 of 19 (31.6%) patients completely responded to steroids.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical and diagnostic characteristics of Hashimoto's encephalopathy: a single-center, retrospective study
evidence.snippet: When HE is suspected, steroids should be considered the first-line treatment.

nodes.description: IVIG is used as an immunomodulatory escalation option when corticosteroids are insufficient or contraindicated, including in pediatric series and refractory cases.
nodes.id: Intravenous Immunoglobulin for Steroid-Refractory or Pediatric Disease

evidence.reference_title: Hashimoto encephalopathy in pediatric patients: Homogeneity in clinical presentation and heterogeneity in antibody titers.
evidence.snippet: one improved with additional intravenous immunoglobulin

evidence.reference_title: Hashimoto's Encephalopathy: Clinical Features, Therapeutic Strategies, and Rehabilitation Approaches
evidence.snippet: alternative immunosuppressive agents or intravenous immunoglobulin may be needed in some cases.

nodes.description: Therapeutic plasma exchange is an escalation option in steroid- and IVIG-refractory SREAT, particularly when a humoral immune process is suspected.

evidence.reference_title: Hashimoto encephalopathy in pediatric patients: Homogeneity in clinical presentation and heterogeneity in antibody titers.
evidence.snippet: The remaining patient did not respond to both treatments and normalized after plasmapheresis.

evidence.reference_title: Hashimoto encephalopathy: a case report and a short revision of current literature.
evidence.snippet: Plasmapheresis was successfully used in steroid non-responders and in case worsened after corticosteroids

nodes.description: True SREAT cases are thought to involve immune-mediated CNS dysfunction with altered mental status, seizures, neuropsychiatric symptoms, sometimes CSF or EEG abnormalities, and frequent response to glucocorticoids. The precise tissue-level mechanism remains unresolved; proposed processes include vasculitic or other inflammatory CNS injury.

evidence.reference_title: Hashimoto's Encephalopathy: Case Series and Literature Review.
evidence.snippet: Available evidences point towards an autoimmune etiology due to vasculitis or other inflammatory process.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical presentations and treatment outcomes of Hashimoto encephalopathy at Siriraj Hospital
evidence.snippet: Most patients responded well to treatment, so clinical suspicion and early diagnosis and treatment will lead to improved patient outcomes.

nodes.description: Additional immunosuppressive agents such as azathioprine, cyclophosphamide, mycophenolate mofetil, or methotrexate are reported in resistant or relapsing disease, but comparative evidence and standardized regimens remain limited.

evidence.reference_title: Hashimoto encephalopathy: a case report and a short revision of current literature.
evidence.snippet: In resistant cases of disease other immunosuppression therapies include plasma exchange, IVIG, methotrexate, azathioprine, cyclophosphamide, and mycophenolate mofetil.

evidence.reference_title: Hashimoto's Encephalopathy: Clinical Features, Therapeutic Strategies, and Rehabilitation Approaches
evidence.snippet: Corticosteroids remain the cornerstone of treatment, although responses vary, and alternative immunosuppressive agents or intravenous immunoglobulin may be needed in some cases.
